assessor_1_raw: The proposal presents good prediction performance and then argues this justifies an intervention program. The critical missing step is causal validation: do discounts prevent churn in this population? The historical correlation between discounts and retention does not answer this because treatment was not randomly assigned.
assessor_2_raw: The selection mechanism for historical discounts (agent judgment about complaints and competitive threats) is entirely different from the model's selection mechanism (behavioral usage features). The two populations are not exchangeable on treatment-response characteristics. An agent-selected discount recipient is, by definition, someone the agent judged likely to respond. A model-selected subscriber may or may not respond.
assessor_3_raw: A well-designed pilot would include a holdout control group among model-selected high-risk subscribers who do not receive discounts. The proposal recommends automatic rollout to the full top decile, which eliminates the possibility of measuring the treatment effect. This makes it impossible to determine post-launch whether the discounts caused any observed churn reduction or whether churn trended down for other reasons.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — scope_intent_abandonment_offer_301 — Ensemble Run 3

Consistent findings across all three assessors and all ensemble runs. Core issues identified unanimously: prediction-intervention scope jump and selection confound in historical offer data.

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS
